---
title: "AI Tools for Data Science and Statistics"
subtitle: "Session 3: PHI, Privacy, and Simulated-Data Workflows"
author: "Erik Westlund"
date: "June 17, 2026"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: true
    css: slides.css
---

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Problem Set 2 & Practice
:::

## Problem Set 2 Practice

- Due to time constraints, we did not practice the modeling workflow
- This is the foundation for problem set 2
- Recommendation: do a quick effort using `practice/02_modeling-workflow`, then the real assignment

## Setting Up Problem Sets/Final Project

- You can use the practice assignments as a guide
- Set up a separate folder outside the course files
- You bring the data (you can copy it out of `data/`) and choose how to organize files
- Email me (ewestlund@jhu.edu) for support

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Context Files Summary
:::

## Context Files

- They are plain text instructions.
- Agents strategically append them to requests so the model sees project guidance.
- `AGENTS.md` (OpenAI/Other models) and `CLAUDE.md` work well for project-wide or subdirectory-wide settings.
- Skills are better for targeted actions or repeated workflows.

## Context Files: How To Use Them

- Hand-crafted context files performed better than AI-generated ones in Gloaguen et al.'s `AGENTS.md` study [@gloaguen2026agentsmd].
- Place must-have constraints in the file before work starts.
- Do not trust the agent to follow every constraint; still review the output.
- When the same papercut happens repeatedly, append a short rule to the context file.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Session 3
:::

## Goals For Session 3

- Separate code movement from data movement
- Use simulated data for development and review
- Identify what information can safely leave a restricted environment only after project-specific approval
- Prepare final-project workflows without exposing restricted data to AI tools

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
AI and Reproducibility
:::

## Code & Reproducibility

- AI writes code for your analysis.
- AI should not "do" the analysis (it will if you ask it to).
- You should **always** be able to re-run your analysis when it's done without any AI tooling.
- Because AI writes the code, you can use it for sensitive work, so long as the AI doesn't touch protected data.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
AI & Protected Data
:::

## Core Principle

Code can move across computing environments; protected data cannot.

## What Counts As Protected?

It depends on the law and your institution's rules. Often:

- Direct identifiers
- Dates, geography, rare conditions, and small cells that can become identifying
- Free text
- Linkable combinations of variables
- Restricted-use datasets even when they do not look individually identifying
- Project-specific metadata covered by agreements or policy

## Agent Risk Surface

- A chat tool sees what you paste or upload.
- An agent can inspect files it has permission to read.
- If any data files are in the workspace, assume the agent has access to them.

## Safe Workflow 

1. Work with restricted data only in the approved environment.
2. Extract only approved structural information.
3. Develop code outside the restricted environment using simulated data.
4. Review generated code before it returns to the restricted environment.
5. Run reviewed code on real data only where real data are allowed.
6. Export only approved aggregate outputs.

## Make It Impossible for AI to See Protected Data

- It is best that it is physically impossible for the agent to access protected information.
- Use a secure enclave for real data access (e.g., SAFE Desktop at Hopkins).
- Set up a virtual machine witht the protected data on it, where no agents could ever access it, regardless of permissions.

## Use Git To Your Advantage

- Keep your code in a git repository
- Use `.gitignore` to exclude the data directory; place your sim data here.
- `git commit` and `git push` from the AI-agent location
- `git pull` from the secure location
- Git helps quickly move code from place to place.

## Workflow & Scientific Advantages

- Using simulated data is not just a workaround an annoyance.
- It can save you a lot of bureaucratic pain with IT, data transfer, etc.
- Simulating data teaches you about the data structure and helps you do good science.

  - Lets you determine what variables are related, and how strong that relationship is
  - This helps you test your statistical models
  - Are they recovering the correct estimates? Handling confounding?

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Protected Data Workflow Example
:::

## Two-Folder Exercise

- `real-data/`: real data and metadata extraction; no AI.
- `sim-data/`: copied metadata, simulated data generation, and AI-assisted notebooks.

. . .

The class data set is not protected, but we will pretend it is.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Real Data Side
:::

## Extract Structural Data

- Start where the real data are allowed.
- Run `extract-data-info.R` without AI assistance.
- Capture exact column names and R data types.
- For continuous variables, capture summary statistics.
- For categorical variables, capture approved levels and distributions.
- Review `real-data-metadata/` before copying anything to `sim-data/real-data-metadata/`.


## Structural Information

Structural information may include, when allowed:

- Variable names
- Variable types
- Value labels
- Allowed categories
- Plausible ranges
- Missing-value codes
- Broad missingness patterns
- Non-disclosive row and column counts

## Capturing Data Structure

- You won't always have access to the real data.
- There is nothing magical about `extract-data-info.R`.
- You can often get what you need through codebooks or just communicating with someone who has data access.
- If you fail to completely get variable names, response levels, etc. correct, you can always fix it by hand in the secure environment.
- Bring that code back to your safe, simulated environment and fix your sim data to match.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Practice real data side together 
:::

## Practice

- Ensure you have run `updater.R`

  - Type `source("updater.R")` in the R console
  - Or just run all the code in the file.

- Open the `practice/work/03_simulated-data-workflow/real-data` project file 

  - **RStudio**: load the `.Rproj` file
  - **Positron**: open the `.code-workspace` file

- Run the `extract-data-info.R` script and copy over the contents of `real-data-metadata` to the sim-data project directory.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Sim Data Side
:::

## Generate The Simulated Data

- Use only approved metadata copied into `real-data-metadata/`.
- Ask AI to complete `generate-sim-data.R`.
- Write simulated data to the same file name as the real data.
- Use the same column names and compatible types.
- Getting relationships correct is optional, but can help with table building and modeling. 
- You can extend extract-data-info.R, for example, to extract margins in cross-tabs and correlations in continuous variables.
- Develop notebooks against the simulated stand-in.

## Information To Treat Carefully

- Exact rare category counts
- Extreme values
- Dates and timestamps
- Geographic detail
- Free-text examples
- Unusual variable combinations
- Any output that could identify a person, clinic, provider, school, or small group

## Simulated Data Still Need Review

- De-identification does not guarantee privacy.
- Do not preserve links between original IDs and simulated IDs.
- Break links between people, providers, clinics, households, schools, and dates.
- Avoid free text, rare categories, and unusually specific geography.
- Check whether simulated records recreate recognizable combinations.

## No External Public Repository Requirement

This workflow does not require an external public repository.

Code can move through institution-approved mechanisms such as:

- approved internal repositories
- secure file transfer
- controlled shared drives
- manual code transfer reviewed before use

. . .

Sensitive data cannot leave the approved environment.

## In A Real Project

- You can use a single repository.
- Git-ignore data on both the real-data and sim-data sides.
- Copy only approved metadata into the AI-visible environment.
- Generate simulated data as a complete stand-in.
- Keep file names and column names identical so code moves cleanly.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Practice simulated data side together 
:::

## Practice

- Open the `sim-data` project file 

  - **RStudio**: load the `.Rproj` file
  - **Positron**: open the `.code-workspace` file

- Ensure you have copied over the `real-data-metadata` outputs to the sim-data directory
- Use an AI agent to generate simulated data by pointing it at the metadata. There is a prompt you can use in `practice/prompts/`.


# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Back to the Real Data
:::

## Return To Real Data

- Copy only reviewed code and notebooks back to `real-data/`.
- Do not copy simulated data back as evidence.
- Run notebooks against the real file with the same file name.
- Write final interpretation only after running on real data.
- Export only approved aggregate results.


# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Practice Real Data (part 2) together 
:::

## Practice

- Open the `real-data` project file 

  - **RStudio**: load the `.Rproj` file
  - **Positron**: open the `.code-workspace` file

- Copy over your new notebooks.
- Run them and see if they work.
- Make any fixes necessary by hand or using the agent in the sim-data environment.

# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Checking Your Work
:::

## Validating AI Outputs

- As a first pass, you can use a second instance of a model -- or ideally a different, similarly capable model -- to evaluate the AI-generated code for bugs
- You must then review all code.
- If you do not have the expertise to validate the AI outputs, ask those who do.

## Cross-Check Prompt Example {.smaller}

```text
Review this analysis code as a skeptical statistical collaborator.

Look for:
- privacy or data exposure risks
- logic errors
- incorrect assumptions about variables
- statistical modeling issues
- missing diagnostics or sensitivity checks

Do not rewrite the code. Give me a list of concerns with line references and suggestions for fixing issues.
```

## Second-Pass Caveat

- A second model can catch errors, but it can also invent concerns that are not real.
- Final checks still belong to the analyst: read the code, run it, inspect outputs, and verify against the scientific question.


## Wrap-Up

- Privacy is a workflow constraint, not an afterthought.
- Agents increase the importance of file boundaries and permissions.
- Simulated data support development, not final inference.
- The analyst remains responsible for correctness, privacy, and interpretation.


# {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Final Thoughts
:::

## Reproducibility

- Use AI to generate code, not to do the analysis
- Ensure future users/viewers of your code require zero AI support to work with your project.

## Master Your Tools

- Learn version control software so you can:

  - Use it to enforce data from ending up cloud repositories
  - Commit changes frequently so you can revert when the AI makes mistakes

- Study what the LLM does so you can improve your understanding of technologies you use
- Invest in learning your tools. It will make you a better analyst and better able to harness AI tools

## You Are Responsible

- Disclose AI use where required.
- You are responsible for privacy, correctness, interpretation of your work.

# Contact

- Reach out with questions: ewestlund@jhu.edu
- Thank you!

# References
